text: As the aviation sector accelerates its decarbonisation journey, Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) remains the only viable drop-in solution available today to significantly reduce lifecycle emissions from air travel. This makes it clear that the work cannot wait. Building a resilient SAF ecosystem requires action now—particularly in diversifying feedstocks and advancing multiple production pathways to scale supply sustainably. At Aster, we are excited to begin this journey and to contribute to the solutions alongside our strategic partners. The transition will take collaboration across the entire value chain, and I look forward to the impact we can create together. As highlighted by The Business Times: [SINGAPORE] Green fuel for airplanes has a take-off problem: The raw material commonly used for its production, used cooking oil (UCO), faces supply bottlenecks, while fraud risks abound in the market. This is spurring a race for alternative means of making sustainable aviation fuel, with two new efforts in Singapore. In January, refiner Aster and asset manager Keppel announced that they are exploring the development of a plant on Jurong Island to produce up to 100,000 tonnes of sustainable aviation fuel a year. Separately, Aster and startup Aether Fuels are building another facility to produce green jet fuel on Pulau Bukom, where Aster took over Shell’s refinery last year. The plant is expected to produce 1,600 tonnes of the fuel a year and 400 tonnes of sustainable naphtha. Notably, neither project taps UCO or other fat sources – a production method known as hydroprocessed esters and fatty acids (Hefa). Instead, they seek to produce sustainable aviation fuel with alternative methods. The Jurong Island project – if it proceeds – would tap low-carbon ethanol as a feedstock, making it one of the first commercial plants in Asia for so-called “ethanol-to-jet” (EtJ) fuel. Meanwhile, the Pulau Bukom plant would convert industrial waste gases and biomethane – a gas fuel derived from waste – into sustainable aviation fuel. It would use an enhanced form of the Fischer-Tropsch (FT) process, which involves a series of chemical reactions. With these projects, Aster “aims to strengthen supply chain resilience, diversify feedstock options, and support Singapore’s ambition to be a regional leader in sustainable aviation fuels”, said Rachel Kwan, its general manager for low-carbon solutions and the circular economy. https://lnkd.in/gV9MGQei #SustainableAviationFuel #SAF #Decarbonisation #EnergyTransition #Aviation #Sustainability #Aster

title: Iowa's ag leader: E-15's moment 'finally at hand' - Enterprise Media - New Hampton Tribune
description:
The event included presentations on market conditions, farm finances and agricultural policy, along with lunch.
A major portion of his remarks focused on the potential impact of expanded year-round access to E-15 fuel.
He said Iowa has already expanded access to the fuel through state policy.
Naig also discussed the “Choose Iowa” program launched by the state to promote products produced within Iowa.
He encouraged farmers to review a new Iowa State University tool known as NFACT that analyzes nitrogen fertilizer rates based on field trials and local conditions.
text:
Expanding the availability of E-15 ethanol is one of the quickest and most impactful policy decisions that could improve the farm economy, the Iowa secretary of agriculture told a group of farmers and agricultural professionals last week in Charles City.

Secretary Mike Naig spoke during the First Security Bank & Trust “Field to Finance” agricultural seminar held at the Swartzrock Community Center at the Floyd County Fairgrounds last Wednesday.

The event included presentations on market conditions, farm finances and agricultural policy, along with lunch.

Naig closed the program with a comprehensive update on state and federal issues affecting agriculture, touching on ethanol policy, export markets, conservation practices and legislation currently under discussion at the Iowa Legislature.

A major portion of his remarks focused on the potential impact of expanded year-round access to E-15 fuel.

“Consumers win because you’re paying 10 to 15 cents less per gallon for E-15 over E-10,” Naig said. “Two, it’s cleaner burning if you care about the environment. Three, it’s good for farmers because of the prices paid. And four, it’s domestic energy production, making us less reliant on what’s happening around the world.”

Federal rules currently allow E-15 to be sold in many places but restrict sales year round in some areas, something Naig said discourages retailers in those areas from offering the fuel because they have to switch signage and they worry it will confuse customers.

“It’s not that you can’t sell it around the country today. It’s just that you can’t sell it during the summer months,” he said.

“If we had year-round access nationwide, another two billion bushels of corn would be ground annually,” Naig said.

He said Iowa has already expanded access to the fuel through state policy. The number of Iowa stations offering E-15 has grown from about 330 in 2022 to more than 1,100 today, with additional stations expected to add the blend later this year.

“And the bottom line is, when it’s available, people buy it,” Naig said.

Naig also emphasized the importance of global markets for Iowa agriculture.

“There’s a stat that I love to remind folks: 96% of the world’s consumers live outside of the United States,” he said.

Iowa ranks second only to California in the value of agricultural exports, he said, and the state continues to promote trade through international missions and partnerships.

Over the past year, Naig said he has traveled to Costa Rica, Guatemala, India, Vietnam and Indonesia as part of efforts to expand export opportunities for Iowa agricultural products.

“What we want to do is sell that value-added product – pork, beef, eggs, ethanol, biodiesel, DDGs – something that’s already been processed and we’ve captured value in the U.S., or in Iowa,” Naig said. DDGs are distillers dried grains, a byproduct of ethanol production and a high value animal feed.

Canada and Mexico remain Iowa’s two largest trading partners, he said, with agricultural products regularly shipped by rail and truck to those markets.

Naig also discussed the “Choose Iowa” program launched by the state to promote products produced within Iowa. The program now includes more than 3,000 members ranging from meat producers and processors to food manufacturers and specialty producers.

Naig also addressed several policy issues currently under discussion in the Iowa Legislature, saying, “I really don’t expect us to leave this session without strengthening property rights, and I don’t expect us to leave this session without some form of property tax reform.”

He said Iowans have sent a clear signal they want the bar raised on the use of eminent domain. He also said he would like to see any bill include guaranteed landowner protections – such as compensation for yield loss and drainage tile repair – written into law rather than left to contract negotiations.

During a question-and-answer portion of the program, Naig responded to a question about proposed carbon dioxide pipelines after the questioner said he didn’t think they were needed and proponents were only looking for the federal tax benefits.

Naig said it’s OK to be skeptical, but the need for low-carbon ethanol is being driven by international markets.

Demand for sustainable aviation fuel is coming from travel hubs such as India and the United Kingdom, and represents a genuine global opportunity that extends beyond U.S. policy, Naig said, adding that low carbon sustainable marine fuel could also be a major market.

Iowa, he added, is well-positioned to compete because it already produces corn and soybeans more sustainably and efficiently than anywhere else in the world.

Naig also discussed conservation efforts aimed at improving water quality while maintaining farm productivity.

Before Iowa’s nutrient reduction strategy was introduced in 2013, he said, about 40,000 acres of cover crops were planted in the state. Today that figure has grown to roughly four million acres.

Other practices being expanded include saturated buffers, which allow tile drainage water to move through soil near streams to reduce nitrates before reaching waterways, and nitrate-reducing wetlands.

The state also monitors water quality through a statewide sampling program managed by the Iowa Department of Natural Resources, which Naig said costs about $3 million annually.

He encouraged farmers to review a new Iowa State University tool known as NFACT that analyzes nitrogen fertilizer rates based on field trials and local conditions.

“You don’t want to underapply because you’re leaving yield on the table. You don’t want to overapply because you’re wasting money and you’re losing it to the environment,” Naig said.

title: A man, a plan, a plane fuel - POLITICO
description:
Lawmakers are blaming the Trump administration, which reduced a federal tax credit for sustainable aviation fuel last year amid its larger unraveling of Biden-era clean energy policy.
“Renewable fuel production is not maxed out, and there is meaningful capacity to increase output across renewable fuels, including sustainable aviation fuel,” they said.
“Policies that support the development of sustainable aviation fuel can help strengthen the long-term viability of these investments.” — AN Did someone forward you this newsletter?
More from POLITICO in California POLITICO Pro California policy moves fast, and the stakes are high.
POLITICO Advertising Solutions POLITICO Advertising Solutions helps organizations reach and engage influential California policy and political audiences.
text:
With help from Camille von Kaenel, Tyler Katzenberger and Noah Baustin

UP IN THE AIR: A plan to boost jet fuel tucked in Gov. Gavin Newsom’s budget is triggering warnings of higher gas prices — a threat that would usually be a death knell in an era of cost-consciousness.

But looming behind the proposed tax credit is the fear that without it, California could lose another refinery at a precarious moment for the global fuels market.

Newsom’s budget scheme — which lawmakers will debate during an Assembly hearing Wednesday — would allow fuel producers to reduce their diesel tax payments in exchange for refining more non-petroleum jet fuel, a concept that his finance officials have framed as a boon for the burgeoning clean aviation sector.

But the state’s nonpartisan Legislative Analyst’s Office last week called on lawmakers to reject the idea, warning that it would siphon funding away from transportation projects that rely on diesel tax revenue. And University of California, Berkeley, researchers released a study late last month that estimated the tax credit could inadvertently increase gas prices by 11 to 14 cents per gallon and diesel prices by 12 cents per gallon. (California’s average gasoline price hit $5.30 per gallon Tuesday, amid a global spike linked to the war in Iran.)

Rather than shying away from the tax credit, a bipartisan group of lawmakers is doubling down on their support. The lawmakers — and a coalition of labor unions — say the proposal emerged from negotiations that started last year to prop up Phillips 66’s Bay Area refinery, which is in dire financial straits.

“If we do not protect and defend this tax credit, we will see a closure at Phillips 66 in Rodeo, and we will have job loss, and we will stifle innovation,” said Assemblymember Anamarie Ávila Farías, whose district borders the small Bay Area city.

Phillips 66’s Rodeo Renewable Energy Complex is California’s largest producer of renewable diesel and sustainable aviation fuel, which are refined from plant and animal-based oils, and is an integral part of the state’s strategy to wean the trucking industry and airlines off fossil fuels. Despite producing roughly 50,000 barrels of the increasingly popular fuels daily, the facility has reported hundreds of millions in losses since becoming fully operational in 2024.

Lawmakers are blaming the Trump administration, which reduced a federal tax credit for sustainable aviation fuel last year amid its larger unraveling of Biden-era clean energy policy. Ávila Farías, who led a dozen lawmakers on a tour of the Rodeo refinery in August as part of a pitch for financial help, said Trump’s assault on clean energy and provocation in Iran have buoyed support.

“We’re feeling good about it,” she said. “I can tell you that what’s going on right now in Iran, in the Middle East, heightens the need to have infrastructure here in California.”

Powerful unions have lined up behind the aviation tax credit in recent weeks, writing in letters to Newsom and lawmakers that abandoning the Rodeo refinery would be punishing a facility that employs hundreds of workers and is advancing California’s climate goals.

“I think it would be a big hit to the reputation of the state as we move to decarbonize our energy needs,” said Chris Hannan, president of the State Building and Construction Trades Council of California.

The stiffest source of opposition is coming from within the oil industry itself.

Both the Western States Petroleum Association, which represents major oil producers and refiners, and the California Fuels and Convenience Alliance asked lawmakers to reject the tax credit at a hearing last week, citing the Berkeley study and arguing that the state should focus on maintaining its traditional refineries.

“You’re losing a refinery, likely next month, that provides the fuel that folks use every day,” said Zachary Leary, WSPA’s chief lobbyist, referring to Valero’s Benicia refinery. “And we believe that the state and the Legislature should be focused on preserving and protecting the remaining petroleum refineries.”

A Phillips 66 spokesperson said in a statement that the company can’t speak to WSPA’s motivations for opposing the tax credit but argued that the UC Berkeley study is based on a false assumption that incentivizing aviation fuel production will result in a decline in renewable diesel and throw the market into turmoil.

“Renewable fuel production is not maxed out, and there is meaningful capacity to increase output across renewable fuels, including sustainable aviation fuel,” they said.

The spokesperson, who didn’t answer a question about whether the Rodeo facility is in imminent danger of closing, called the tax plan “the best route forward.”

“Phillips 66 has made significant investments in California that align with the state’s climate and energy goals,” they said. “Policies that support the development of sustainable aviation fuel can help strengthen the long-term viability of these investments.” — AN

Did someone forward you this newsletter? Sign up here!

CAP THE CAP: Gas prices have again turned the political tides against California’s climate regulators.

Politicians from Nevada Gov. Joe Lombardo, a Republican, to moderate in-state Democrats bashed the California Air Resources Board’s proposed new rules for its carbon emissions market ahead of Monday’s midnight deadline for comments.

Lombardo wrote to Newsom urging him to direct CARB to reconsider its draft cap-and-trade rules for their possible impact on already-struggling California refineries, which also provide much of the fuel for Nevada.

“Any major policy change that could alter refinery economics in California must account for the real-world consequences to neighboring states that depend on that infrastructure,” Lombardo wrote. “Given additional tension in the Middle East, the situation is particularly pressing.”

Antonio Villaraigosa, the former Los Angeles mayor now running for California governor, also echoed the call to reconsider, saying in a written statement on Monday “the priority should be making sure our policies improve affordability and economic stability.”

More than a dozen moderate Assemblymembers also chimed in.

“It is now evident that California’s regulatory environment has driven out actors necessary for an affordable energy consumer market,” wrote the group, which includes Assemblymembers Blanca Rubio, Cecilia Aguiar-Curry and 13 others.

The backlash marks a broad reversal from last fall, when the Western States Petroleum Association and the State Building and Construction Trades Council paved the way for the 15-year extension of the cap-and-trade market by removing their opposition to a new state law in the last days of the legislative session. Those groups are now opposing the draft rules, citing the possible impacts to refineries.

The pile-on also echoes the public blowback CARB received in 2024 when it tightened emissions rules for transportation fuels under its low-carbon fuel standard.

CARB is scheduled to vote on the rules in May, making them effective in September, but will consider any changes before a vote. — CvK

SOLAR SADNESS: A California court snuffed out a glimmer of hope for the rooftop solar industry.

The California First Appellate District Court of Appeal issued an opinion Monday striking down a lawsuit that a trio of solar advocates filed against the California Public Utilities Commission challenging the third-generation net metering system, known as NEM 3.0. The agency adopted the framework in 2022 in an effort to make rooftop solar users pay for a larger share of the fixed cost of maintaining the electricity grid. Rooftop solar installations fell significantly in California after the policy went into effect.

It’s the second time the court’s sided with the CPUC, after the California Supreme Court’s ruling last August that kicked it back to the lower court. — NB

CORPORATE CUT: A California lawmaker wants to dramatically reduce utility profits.

Assemblymember Tasha Boerner on Monday introduced amendments to her spot bill AB 1677 (which may still not be in print by the time you’re reading this). The bill would place a ceiling on the return on equity that utilities are allowed to pass onto their shareholders of four percent above the rate on long-term debt issued by the federal government. That would be 8.8 percent as of today, a significant drop from the current rate, which ranges from 9.8 to 10 percent.

She also amended another spot bill, AB 1774, to propose a mandated independent audit of the last five years of utility wildfire mitigation spending before the CPUC could authorize California utilities to collect more ratepayer funds for wildfire work. — NB

CONGESTION PRICING — Los Angeles airport commissioners voted Tuesday to hike fees for ride-hailing apps, limos and taxis at Los Angeles International Airport, potentially making travel more expensive for global athletes, spectators and world leaders slated to fly in for the World Cup and Olympics.

The six-member board of commissioners unanimously approved the fee hike despite opposition from 10 LA-area lawmakers, led by Assemblymember Tina McKinnor, who wrote in a joint letter Monday that it would “make rideshare access at LAX among the most expensive of any major airport globally.”

Access fees for ride-hailing trips to and from LAX are currently $4 or $5 per trip, depending on the type of transportation. Tuesday’s vote increases the base fee to $6 and tees up airport staff to levy a separate, $6 charge on top of that increase for trips picking up or dropping off riders immediately outside airport terminals once LAX’s free Automated People Mover, dubbed “SkyLink,” opens.

Ride-hailing giant Uber sent an email alert to consumers Monday that urged them to speak out against the proposed increase.

Airport staff and commissioners backing the plan said that tech giants like Uber and Lyft can and should eat the fee increases. Further, they said, their plan will generate millions in revenue for infrastructure improvements and reduce snaking traffic at the bustling aviation hub by nudging travelers onto SkyLink, which will connect terminals to a nearby metro station if it opens as planned later this year.

“Concentrating all of this activity in a single, two-level terminal loop is no longer sustainable, and it hasn’t been for a long time,” said David Reich, Los Angeles World Airports deputy executive director for mobility strategy. — TK

— Oil prices whipsawed after Energy Secretary Chris Wright said — and the White House subsequently took back — that the U.S. Navy escorted an oil tanker near Iran.

— Industry observers say that it could be a boon for the electric vehicle market if gasoline prices continue climbing.

— A heat wave with temperatures up to 30 degrees above normal is forecast for California starting later this week.

text:
North Dakota Farmers Union President Matt Perdue represented National Farmers Union in a hearing today before the U.S. Senate Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry, testifying on ways to increase domestic consumption of agricultural products.

Perdue cited investment in value-added processing and renewable fuels markets, expansion of local and regional supply chains, and addressing competition as ways to increase domestic markets. “Strengthening domestic markets is not merely an economic choice but a prerequisite for the long-term survival of family farmers and rural communities,” he testified.

In North Dakota, Perdue said value-added processing acts as a hedge against volatile global commodity prices and creates opportunities to produce and market different commodities. He emphasized the vital role biofuels play in strengthening farm income. “By transforming crops like corn, soybeans and canola into renewable energy, farmers capture added value from every bushel while supporting domestic energy security.”

Specifically, expanding the market for biofuels, including year-round sales of 15% ethanol blended gasoline and higher targets for bio-based diesel, would increase demand for U.S.-grown crops, Perdue testified. He also highlighted opportunities for farmers with the shift toward sustainable aviation fuel.

Perdue asked Congress to invest in programs that support local and regional food procurement programs to help farmers expand and build new markets. He encouraged continued investment in local and regional livestock processing, which has created “new market outlets for producers and [is] boosting consumers’ access to locally produced and processed beef.”

He emphasized that new market opportunities must be fair and transparent, so producers have an opportunity to compete. “It is critical that Congress and the administration work together to enforce and enhance our antitrust and pro-competition laws,” Perdue stated. “Farmers today must buy from and sell into highly consolidated and uncompetitive markets.”

Perdue testified alongside representatives from the American Farm Bureau Federation, American Soybean Association, International Fresh Produce Association, National Corn Growers Association, and National Cotton Council.

To view all testimony, go to: agriculture.senate.gov/hearings.

text:
FRANKFORT, Ky. (LEX 18) — Kentucky's farmland could soon help power the aviation industry. That's the pitch from state and industry leaders backing House Bill 545, a proposal that would offer tax incentives to produce and blend sustainable aviation fuel made from Kentucky-grown crops.

Supporters say the bill could deliver major benefits for both aviation and agriculture, and that the state can't afford to wait.

"This is not a 'Can we or should we,' this is a must for the state of Kentucky," Agriculture Commissioner Jonathan Shell said.

House Bill 545 would incentivize the production of sustainable aviation fuel using Kentucky-grown feedstocks like corn and soybeans. Proponents say the demand is already there — airlines are actively looking for reliable domestic supplies.

Speaker Pro Tempore David Meade said Kentucky risks being left behind if it doesn't act.

"Airlines across the country are actively searching for reliable supplies of this fuel, cargo carriers and passenger airlines are signing long term agreements, and the question isn't whether the industry will grow, it's whether or not Kentucky will be a part of that and capture the investment or if we're gonna watch it go to other states," Meade said.

Larry Krauter, CEO of Cincinnati-Northern Kentucky International Airport, said the economic opportunity is significant.

"$100.3 million is the estimated economic impact of one European commercial flight destination from CVG. When you think about the opportunity to leverage the connection through air service development, we must have this tool," Krauter said.

The benefits, supporters say, extend well beyond the runway. Agriculture leaders believe the bill would open new markets for both seasoned and beginning farmers, making their operations more profitable.

Eddie Melton, president of the Kentucky Farm Bureau said the goal is straightforward.

"Making our farms more profitable every day is what this is about," Melton said.

Row crop farmers could see the biggest gains, since corn and soybeans are among the primary feedstocks used to produce the fuel. Shell said Kentucky currently has more agricultural supply than demand — and that this bill could help rebalance that equation.

"This has an opportunity to re-correct the market place so our farmers can produce and get higher prices and more acreages available for them to produce," Shell said.

Advocates point to Kentucky's history of using incentives to launch biodiesel and ethanol industries, along with its existing cargo hubs and logistics networks, as reasons the state is well-positioned to lead.

Shell said the window to act is now.

"If we don't come out of this session with an incentive and with this policy work in the right way, we'll look back in 20 years and wonder why we weren't the first to market," Shell said.

Meade said it's late in the session, meaning a standalone bill likely won't move forward. Instead, he said the proposal could be folded into the larger revenue bill.
